items.q: What is the difference between strength and power?
items.correct: Strength is maximum force; power is force times speed of movement.
items.random_wrong: Strength is for the upper body, power for the lower.
items.why: Students conflate strength and power. A strong person can lift heavy slowly; a powerful athlete generates force QUICKLY (sprinter, shot-putter). Power requires both force and velocity.

items.q: What is the difference between aerobic and muscular endurance?
items.correct: Aerobic endurance is the cardiovascular system's ability to supply oxygen; muscular endurance is a muscle's ability to contract repeatedly without fatigue.
items.misconception: They are different names for the same component.
items.random_wrong: Aerobic is for legs, muscular for arms.
items.why: Students treat both as 'being able to keep going'. Aerobic endurance is WHOLE-BODY cardio capacity; muscular endurance is LOCALISED to specific muscles (e.g., sit-up reps).

items.q: What does the 'S' in SPORT stand for?
items.correct: Specificity - training should match the demands of the sport.
items.why: Students guess fitness components. SPORT = Specificity, Progression, Overload, Reversibility, Tedium (or Time). Specificity means training matches what the athlete needs (sprinter doesn't train like a marathoner).

items.q: What does the 'I' in FITT stand for?
items.why: Students guess. FITT = Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type - the variables you adjust to apply progressive overload. Intensity = how hard (e.g., % of 1RM or heart rate).

items.q: Which energy system fuels a marathon?
items.why: Students associate hard exercise with anaerobic systems. Long-duration steady-state work uses AEROBIC metabolism (oxygen + carbs/fats) - efficient but slower to ramp up. Anaerobic systems dominate brief intense efforts.

items.q: How long does the ATP-PC system last at maximum intensity?
items.why: Students overestimate the ATP-PC system. It provides instant energy but stores deplete in ~10 seconds (one sprint, one heavy lift). After that, glycolysis or aerobic systems take over.

items.q: Which type of muscle is under voluntary control?
items.why: Students think all muscles can be controlled if we try hard enough. Cardiac and smooth muscle are involuntary - you can't consciously beat your heart. Only skeletal muscle responds to conscious commands.

items.q: What sport is best suited to type I (slow-twitch) fibres?
items.why: Students confuse which fibres suit which event. Type I = slow, fatigue-resistant, aerobic (endurance). Type II = fast, powerful, anaerobic (sprint/power). Distance runners are type-I-dominant.

items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.q: Most movements in the human body use which class of lever?
items.why: Students assume the most familiar (first class - seesaw) is most common. Actually most body levers are third class (effort between fulcrum and load) - like the biceps curl. They sacrifice mechanical advantage for speed/range.

items.q: What is cardiac output?
items.correct: Volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute (stroke volume x heart rate).
items.misconception: The volume of blood in the heart at any moment.
items.why: Students confuse stroke volume (one beat) with cardiac output (per minute total). CO = SV x HR. A 70mL stroke at 70bpm = 4.9L/min cardiac output.

items.q: What is tidal volume?
items.correct: Volume of air inhaled or exhaled in one normal breath at rest.
items.misconception: The maximum air your lungs can hold.
items.random_wrong: Air left in the lungs after maximum exhalation.
items.why: Students confuse tidal volume (resting breath, ~0.5L) with vital capacity (max breath, ~4-5L). Different lung volumes serve different purposes - tidal is the normal small ebb and flow.

items.q: Where is glycogen primarily stored?
items.why: Students confuse storage with circulation. Blood carries glucose; muscles and liver store glycogen (the storage form). Liver glycogen regulates blood sugar; muscle glycogen fuels local contraction.

items.q: What causes muscle soreness 1-2 days after intense exercise?
items.correct: Microscopic muscle damage (DOMS), not lactic acid.
items.misconception: Lactic acid still in the muscles.
items.why: Students believe the myth that lactic acid causes soreness. Lactate clears within an hour. Delayed onset muscle soreness (DOMS) is caused by microtrauma from eccentric contractions, peaking 24-72 hours later.

items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, when is performance best?
items.correct: At an optimal moderate level of arousal.
items.why: Students assume 'more psyched up = better'. The inverted-U shows performance rises with arousal up to an optimum, then DECLINES with excess arousal (the athlete chokes). The optimum varies by skill.

items.q: What does the 'M' in SMART goals stand for?
items.why: Students guess from common psychology terms. SMART = Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Realistic (or Relevant), Time-bound. Measurable means progress can be tracked (e.g., '5 km in 25 minutes' not 'get faster').

items.q: When you flex your elbow, which muscle is the agonist?
items.why: Students confuse agonist (the prime mover doing the action) with antagonist (the opposing muscle that relaxes). In elbow flexion, biceps is the agonist; triceps is the antagonist (and vice versa for extension).

items.q: What is the rough formula for maximum heart rate?
items.why: Students misremember the formula. 220 - age is the classic estimate (rough; varies individually). A 20-year-old's estimated max HR is around 200 bpm; an 80-year-old's is around 140.

items.q: What does plyometric training develop?
items.why: Students lump all training together. Plyometrics specifically targets POWER - explosive jumps and bounds use rapid eccentric-then-concentric muscle action, training the stretch-shortening cycle for fast force production.

items.q: Is a tennis serve a closed or open skill?
items.correct: Closed - performed in a predictable, self-paced environment.
items.misconception: Open - because tennis is a fast-paced sport.
items.why: Students judge by the sport, not the moment. The serve happens at the server's pace with a stationary ball - closed. The return of serve is open. Skill classification depends on the SPECIFIC action's environment.

items.q: What is progressive overload?
items.correct: Gradually increasing training demands so the body keeps adapting.
items.misconception: Training as hard as possible immediately.
items.why: Students think 'overload' means 'maximum'. It means slightly exceeding current capacity - too much causes injury; too little stalls progress. The key word is PROGRESSIVE.

items.q: What does EPO (erythropoietin) do?
items.correct: Increases red blood cell production, improving oxygen-carrying capacity.
items.why: Students assume all PEDs work the same way. EPO is specifically for endurance - more red cells = more oxygen to working muscles. Anabolic steroids do something different (promote protein synthesis).

items.q: What is intrinsic feedback?
items.correct: Information the performer senses from their own body during/after movement.
items.why: Students reverse the meaning. Intrinsic = from WITHIN (kinesthetic awareness, feel); extrinsic = from OUTSIDE (coach feedback, video, statistics). Experienced performers rely more on intrinsic feedback.
